markdown.markdown:
The CNO branch is the deterministic part of the decomposition:

$$
\mathbf{x}_{HR} = \boldsymbol{\mu}(\mathbf{x}_{LR}) + \mathbf{r}
$$

It estimates the large-scale high-resolution field:

$$
\boldsymbol{\mu} =
\mathbf{x}_{LR} + f_{\theta}^{CNO}(\mathbf{x}_{LR})
$$

In this project the CNO is trained as a residual super-resolution model:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{CNO} =
\left\| f_{\theta}^{CNO}(\mathbf{x}_{LR})
- (\mathbf{x}_{HR} - \mathbf{x}_{LR}) \right\|_2^2
$$

The motivation is pragmatic: CNO should explain the deterministic, stable part of the correction, while the
generative model is reserved for the uncertain residual.

## Why CNO

CNO is a neural-operator-inspired architecture. The useful idea for OceanLens is that it treats the input and output
as spatial fields rather than isolated pixels. That is closer to what ocean downscaling needs: a mapping between
functions on a grid.

::: {.reference-grid}
::: {.reference-card}
### Paper

[Convolutional Neural Operators for robust and accurate learning of PDEs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2302.01178)
:::

::: {.reference-card}
### Official repository

[camlab-ethz/ConvolutionalNeuralOperator](https://github.com/camlab-ethz/ConvolutionalNeuralOperator)
:::

::: {.reference-card}
### OceanLens implementation

`src/oceanlens/models/cno.py`
:::
:::

## Inputs And Outputs

| Item | Meaning |
|---|---|
| Input | Upsampled LR field on the HR grid |
| Channels | `thetao`, `so`, `zos`, `uo`, `vo` |
| Target | `HR - LR` |
| Output | Deterministic residual correction |
| Final mean | `mu = LR + CNO(LR)` |

## Interpretation

If CNO is good, `mu` should be smooth, stable and physically plausible at large scale. It should not be expected
to sample all possible eddy-level details. That is why the second branch exists.

## Role In OceanLens

The CNO branch is the deterministic part of the decomposition:

$$
\mathbf{x}_{HR} = \boldsymbol{\mu}(\mathbf{x}_{LR}) + \mathbf{r}
$$

It estimates the large-scale high-resolution field:

$$
\boldsymbol{\mu} =
\mathbf{x}_{LR} + f_{\theta}^{CNO}(\mathbf{x}_{LR})
$$

In this project the CNO is trained as a residual super-resolution model:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{CNO} =
\left\| f_{\theta}^{CNO}(\mathbf{x}_{LR})
- (\mathbf{x}_{HR} - \mathbf{x}_{LR}) \right\|_2^2
$$

The motivation is pragmatic: CNO should explain the deterministic, stable part of the correction, while the
generative model is reserved for the uncertain residual.

## Why CNO

CNO is a neural-operator-inspired architecture. The useful idea for OceanLens is that it treats the input and output
as spatial fields rather than isolated pixels. That is closer to what ocean downscaling needs: a mapping between
functions on a grid.

::: {.reference-grid}
::: {.reference-card}
### Paper

[Convolutional Neural Operators for robust and accurate learning of PDEs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2302.01178)

::: {.reference-card}

| Item | Meaning |
|---|---|
| Input | Upsampled LR field on the HR grid |
| Channels | `thetao`, `so`, `zos`, `uo`, `vo` |
| Target | `HR - LR` |
| Output | Deterministic residual correction |
| Final mean | `mu = LR + CNO(LR)` |
